title: 2В  Chapter 2: Decomposition, Smoothing, and Business Cycle Analysis
section: 2.3 The Business Cycle as a Decomposition Problem
text:
2.3 The Business Cycle as a Decomposition Problem

What the Business Cycle Is вЂ” and Is Not
The business cycle is one of the most studied and debated concepts in macroeconomics, yet it is surprisingly hard to define precisely. The phrase conjures images of booms and busts, expansions and recessions, the rhythmic up-and-down of economic activity. But this intuitive picture conceals a fundamental ambiguity: up and down relative to what?
If we mean relative to last period, we are looking at growth rates вЂ” and growth rates are always fluctuating, even in a healthy economy. If we mean relative to a fixed level, we have the problem that the economy trends upward over time and never returns to its past level. The only economically coherent benchmark is the trend вЂ” the economyвЂ™s long-run potential path вЂ” and deviations from that trend are what we mean by the business cycle.
This definition has an important implication: the business cycle is not a natural object sitting in the data waiting to be discovered. It is a residual вЂ” what is left after we remove the trend. And because different methods produce different trends, they produce different business cycles. This is not a methodological embarrassment; it is an economically meaningful fact. Whether the 1990s boom appears large or modest, whether the 2001 recession looks shallow or deep, depends directly on how we model the trend that GDP is deviating from.


Linear and Quadratic Detrending
The simplest trend model is a deterministic time trend: we assume the log of output grows at a constant rate over time, and any deviation from that line is cyclical. This is the linear detrending approach. We estimate it by regressing log GDP on a linear time index \(t\):
\[\log y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \varepsilon_t \tag{2.1}\]
The OLS regression finds the line \(\hat{\alpha} + \hat{\beta} t\) that best fits the log series in a least-squares sense. The residual \(\hat{\varepsilon}_t = \log y_t - \hat{\alpha} - \hat{\beta} t\) is the cyclical component вЂ” the percentage deviation of output from its estimated linear trend.
A natural extension allows for a changing growth rate by including a quadratic term:
\[\log y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \gamma t^2 + \varepsilon_t \tag{2.2}\]
The quadratic trend can accommodate the well-documented postwar productivity slowdown вЂ” the fact that US potential output appears to have grown faster in the 1950sвЂ“60s than in the decades since.
Both approaches embed a strong assumption: that the trend is purely deterministic. Shocks to output are transitory вЂ” the economy always reverts to the same fixed path. We found evidence in Chapter 1 that log real GDP is \(I(1)\): its unit root means shocks are at least partly permanent. Imposing a deterministic trend on a unit-root process is a form of misspecification. The trend will chase the data, and the extracted cycle will be contaminated by whatever the trend misses. Still, as a simple first pass вЂ” and as a baseline against which richer methods can be compared вЂ” linear and quadratic detrending are worth knowing.


Show code вЂ” Linear and quadratic detrending
log_sa = gdp["Log SA"].dropna()
T      = len(log_sa)
t      = np.arange(1, T + 1)

# OLS detrending
X_lin  = np.column_stack([np.ones(T), t])
X_quad = np.column_stack([np.ones(T), t, t**2])

b_lin  = np.linalg.lstsq(X_lin,  log_sa.values, rcond=None)[0]
b_quad = np.linalg.lstsq(X_quad, log_sa.values, rcond=None)[0]

trend_lin  = X_lin  @ b_lin
trend_quad = X_quad @ b_quad

cycle_lin  = log_sa.values - trend_lin
cycle_quad = log_sa.values - trend_quad

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(6, 5), sharex=True)

ax = axes[0]
ax.plot(log_sa.index, log_sa.values,  color=EO_CHARCOAL,   lw=0.9, label="Log SA GDP")
ax.plot(log_sa.index, trend_lin,       color=EO_COPPER,     lw=1.1, ls="--", label="Linear trend")
ax.plot(log_sa.index, trend_quad,      color=EO_SKYBLUE,    lw=1.1, ls="--", label="Quadratic trend")
shade_recessions(ax, start=PLOT_START)
ax.set_title("Log Level and Fitted Trends")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=6)
eo_style_ax(ax)

ax = axes[1]
ax.plot(log_sa.index, cycle_lin  * 100, color=EO_COPPER,  lw=0.9, label="Linear cycle")
ax.plot(log_sa.index, cycle_quad * 100, color=EO_SKYBLUE, lw=0.9, label="Quadratic cycle")
ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, lw=0.6, ls=":")
shade_recessions(ax, start=PLOT_START)
ax.set_title("Cyclical Component (% deviation from trend)")
ax.set_ylabel("Percent")
ax.legend(loc="lower left", fontsize=6)
eo_style_ax(ax)

eo_suptitle(fig, "Linear and Quadratic Detrending вЂ” Log SA Real GDP")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








FigureВ 2.3: Linear and quadratic detrending of log SA real GDP, 1947вЂ“2024. Top panel: the log level with both fitted trends. Bottom panel: the cyclical components (residuals). The linear trend forces US output to revert to a single long-run growth rate, making the 1970s slowdown appear as an extended negative cycle and the 1990s boom appear as a substantial positive one. The quadratic trend captures the productivity slowdown by allowing the growth rate to change, shifting the extracted cycle. NBER recession dates are shaded in both panels.





The bottom panel illustrates the dependence problem clearly. The choice of trend determines the cycle. The linear trend, by forcing a single constant growth rate on the entire postwar period, interprets the 1970s as a sustained negative deviation and the late 1990s as a large positive one. The quadratic trend, by allowing the growth rate itself to change, recalibrates both episodes. Neither is wrong in a technical sense вЂ” both minimize least squares on their respective parameterizations вЂ” but they tell quite different stories about the economyвЂ™s cyclical position at any given date.


The HP Filter
By far the most widely used tool for business cycle extraction in macroeconomics is the Hodrick-Prescott filter, introduced in a 1980 working paper (published in 1997). Its central appeal is that it solves a transparent optimization problem: find a smooth trend that tracks the data without following every wiggle.
Formally, given a time series \(\{y_t\}_{t=1}^T\), the HP filter finds the trend \(\{\tau_t\}\) that minimizes:
\[\sum_{t=1}^{T}(y_t - \tau_t)^2 + \lambda \sum_{t=2}^{T-1}\bigl[(\tau_{t+1} - \tau_t) - (\tau_t - \tau_{t-1})\bigr]^2 \tag{2.3}\]
The first sum penalizes deviations of the trend from the data вЂ” it pushes the trend toward the series. The second sum penalizes changes in the growth rate of the trend вЂ” it pushes the trend toward a straight line. The scalar \(\lambda > 0\) governs the trade-off between these two objectives.






NoteDefinition 2.2 вЂ” The HP Filter



The Hodrick-Prescott filter extracts a trend \(\{\hat{\tau}_t\}\) from \(\{y_t\}\) by solving:
\[\min_{\{\tau_t\}} \left\{ \sum_{t=1}^{T}(y_t - \tau_t)^2 + \lambda \sum_{t=2}^{T-1}\bigl[(\tau_{t+1} - \tau_t) - (\tau_t - \tau_{t-1})\bigr]^2 \right\}\]
The smoothing parameter \(\lambda\) controls the flexibility of the trend. As \(\lambda \to 0\), the trend passes through every data point (no smoothing). As \(\lambda \to \infty\), the trend approaches a straight line (maximum smoothing). The conventional choices, due to Hodrick and Prescott, are \(\lambda = 1600\) for quarterly data, \(\lambda = 14400\) for monthly data, and \(\lambda = 100\) for annual data.
The cyclical component is defined as \(\hat{c}_t = y_t - \hat{\tau}_t\).


The second term in the objective penalizes \((\tau_{t+1} - \tau_t) - (\tau_t - \tau_{t-1})\), which is the second difference of the trend вЂ” the change-in-change, or discrete approximation to the second derivative. Penalizing this means the filter resists trends that accelerate or decelerate rapidly. The solution to the optimization problem is linear in \(y\) and can be written in matrix form as \(\hat{\tau} = (I + \lambda K'K)^{-1} y\), where \(K\) is the \((T-2) \times T\) second-difference matrix. In practice, statsmodels.tsa.filters.hp_filter.hpfilter computes this for us.
The HP filter became the de facto standard in the business cycle literature вЂ” used in hundreds of empirical papers, taught in every graduate macroeconomics course вЂ” because it is simple to apply, transparent in its objective, and produces cycles that broadly match the NBER recession dates. But it has also attracted serious criticism, most forcefully from James Hamilton.


HamiltonвЂ™s Critique and Alternative
In a 2018 paper in the Review of Economics and Statistics, James Hamilton argued that the HP filter has three fundamental problems that render its output unreliable for business cycle analysis.
First, the HP filter generates spurious cycles even when the true data-generating process has none. Hamilton showed that applying the HP filter to a pure random walk вЂ” a process with no cyclical component by construction вЂ” produces a cyclical residual with the same statistical properties as what practitioners report as business cycles. The filter manufactures structure from noise.
Second, the HP filter produces substantial end-point bias: the trend estimate near the most recent observations is especially sensitive to the choice of \(\lambda\), because the filter has less data on one side to anchor the trend. In real-time applications вЂ” precisely when policymakers need reliable estimates вЂ” the HP cycle is least trustworthy.
Third, the second-difference penalty implicitly assumes the trend is integrated of order two (\(I(2)\)). But log real GDP is \(I(1)\), not \(I(2)\). The HP filter is solving the right optimization problem for the wrong class of process.
HamiltonвЂ™s alternative is disarmingly simple. He proposes projecting \(y_{t+h}\) on the four most recent values of \(y\):
\[y_{t+h} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 y_t + \beta_2 y_{t-1} + \beta_3 y_{t-2} + \beta_4 y_{t-3} + \varepsilon_{t+h} \tag{2.4}\]
and using the OLS residual as the cyclical component. For quarterly GDP, Hamilton recommends \(h = 8\) (two years ahead). The residual \(\hat{\varepsilon}_{t+h}\) captures the part of \(y_{t+h}\) that is not predictable from the recent history of the series вЂ” which is a natural, model-free definition of a cyclical deviation.
This approach has several advantages over HP. It is robust to the order of integration of \(y_t\) вЂ” it works whether the series is \(I(1)\) or trend-stationary. It has no end-point bias, since both ends of the sample lose observations symmetrically. It does not require choosing a smoothing parameter. And it does not generate spurious cycles from noise.
The main disadvantage is interpretive: the Hamilton filter extracts a cycle at the frequency \(1/h\), which for \(h = 8\) quarters corresponds to a two-year horizon. This is somewhat shorter than the conventional NBER business cycle, and it misses lower-frequency variation that the HP filter would retain. As with every decomposition choice, this is a feature вЂ” HamiltonвЂ™s filter is asking a specific question вЂ” but one that users should be aware of.


Show code вЂ” Four-method cycle comparison
log_sa_full = gdp["Log SA"].dropna()

# в”Ђв”Ђ HP Filter в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
cycle_hp, trend_hp = hpfilter(log_sa_full, lamb=1600)

# в”Ђв”Ђ Hamilton Filter в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
h_ham = 8
lags  = 4
y_arr = log_sa_full.values
n     = len(y_arr)

t_start = lags - 1
t_end   = n - 1 - h_ham

dep  = y_arr[(t_start + h_ham):(t_end + h_ham + 1)]
regs = np.column_stack([
    np.ones(len(dep)),
    y_arr[t_start:t_end + 1],
    y_arr[t_start - 1:t_end],
    y_arr[t_start - 2:t_end - 1],
    y_arr[t_start - 3:t_end - 2],
])
b_ham   = np.linalg.lstsq(regs, dep, rcond=None)[0]
resid   = dep - regs @ b_ham

ham_dates = log_sa_full.index[(t_start + h_ham):(t_end + h_ham + 1)]
cycle_ham = pd.Series(resid, index=ham_dates)

# в”Ђв”Ђ STL Irregular в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
# Use the SA series for a fair comparison (no seasonal contamination)
stl_sa     = STL(log_sa_full, period=4, seasonal=7, robust=True).fit()
cycle_stl  = pd.Series(stl_sa.resid, index=log_sa_full.index)

# в”Ђв”Ђ Linear detrend cycle (already computed above) в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
cycle_lin_s = pd.Series(cycle_lin, index=log_sa_full.index)

# в”Ђв”Ђ Plot в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
labels = [
    "HP Filter (О» = 1600)",
    "Hamilton Filter (h = 8 quarters)",
    "Linear Detrend",
    "STL Irregular",
]
cycles = [cycle_hp * 100, cycle_ham * 100, cycle_lin_s * 100, cycle_stl * 100]
colors = [EO_COPPER, EO_SKYBLUE, EO_SAGE, EO_TERRACOTTA]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 1, figsize=(6, 8), sharex=True)

for ax, cyc, label, color in zip(axes, cycles, labels, colors):
    ax.plot(cyc.index, cyc.values, color=color, lw=0.85)
    ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, lw=0.6, ls=":")
    ax.fill_between(cyc.index, cyc.values, 0,
                    where=(cyc.values < 0),
                    color=color, alpha=0.22)
    shade_recessions(ax, start=PLOT_START)
    ax.set_title(label)
    ax.set_ylabel("% dev.")
    eo_style_ax(ax)

end_yr = gdp.index[-1].year
eo_suptitle(fig, f"Business Cycle вЂ” Four Methods, {gdp.index[0].year}вЂ“{end_yr}")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








FigureВ 2.4: Business cycle components from four methods applied to log SA real GDP. HP filter (О»=1600, copper); Hamilton filter (h=8, blue); linear detrend (sage); STL irregular (terracotta). NBER recessions are shaded. The Hamilton panel is blank before 1949 Q4 by construction: the filter requires h=8 quarters of lead time and four lags of the series, so the first cycle estimate is only available once those observations exist. This is not a deficiency вЂ” it is an honest acknowledgement that the method needs a minimum data window. The STL irregular is the noisiest series but the most unfiltered: it has not been smoothed by any bandwidth parameter, so every quarterвЂ™s deviation from trend is shown at full resolution.





The four-panel figure is the visual centrepiece of this section, and it repays careful reading. Starting from the top:
The HP filter produces the smoothest cycle. Its trend adapts continuously to the data, which is its strength and its weakness simultaneously. Notice that near the end of the sample the HP cycle tends to be smaller in absolute value than the other methods: with no future data to anchor the right end of the trend, the filter lets the trend drift toward recent observations, compressing the apparent cycle. This is end-point bias in action, and the next subsection shows its policy consequences in detail.
The Hamilton filter panel is blank for the first several quarters. This is not a programming error вЂ” it is an honest feature of the method. The filter requires \(h = 8\) quarters of lead time (it predicts \(y_{t+8}\) from \(y_t\) through \(y_{t-3}\)) plus four lags to form the right-hand side of the regression, so the first residual is only available once those observations exist. Both ends of the sample lose \(h + \text{lags}\) observations symmetrically, which is exactly why the filter has no end-point bias: it treats the beginning and end of the sample identically. When data exists, the Hamilton cycle tracks NBER recessions reliably and generally agrees with HP on the direction of the cycle, while disagreeing on amplitude.
The linear detrend cycle is the most volatile of the smooth methods. By constraining the trend to a single straight line fitted over the entire postwar period, it forces every deviation from that line вЂ” including the entire productivity slowdown of the 1970s вЂ” into the cyclical component. The result is a large, persistent negative cycle through the 1970s and a correspondingly large positive cycle through the late 1990s that almost certainly overstates the boom.
The STL irregular is the most honest series in the figure. Because STLвЂ™s Loess trend is highly flexible and its seasonal factors are estimated rather than imposed, the remainder captures genuine high-frequency variation without imposing any smoothness on the cycle itself. It is noisier than the other three methods вЂ” individual quarter-to-quarter movements are fully preserved вЂ” but precisely because of this, it is less likely to manufacture cycle patterns that are artifacts of the trend specification. The noise is the signal: it tells us that the вЂњbusiness cycleвЂќ in the other panels has already been smoothed by choices about О», h, or the regression horizon before we even look at it.


The HP FilterвЂ™s End-Point Problem: A Policy Illustration
The end-point bias of the HP filter is not merely a statistical curiosity. It can lead a policymaker who relies on real-time HP estimates to draw the opposite conclusion from the one the data, in hindsight, support. The pre-2008 period offers a vivid illustration.
Run the HP filter on log SA real GDP using only data available through 2007 Q4 вЂ” the last quarter before the financial crisis was officially dated as having begun. The filter, seeing a long expansion with no future data to вЂњknowвЂќ about the coming collapse, interprets much of the 2004вЂ“2007 growth as trend. The result: a modest positive cycle, perhaps \(+1\) to \(+2\%\). The economy looks roughly at potential, perhaps slightly above. A policymaker reading this in early 2008 might conclude that no major stimulus is needed вЂ” the economy is running near its long-run path.
Now run the same HP filter on data through 2024 Q4. With the Great Recession and its aftermath now in the sample, the filterвЂ™s trend for 2007 is pulled downward вЂ” the full-sample trend is lower than the real-time trend, because the filter now вЂњknowsвЂќ what came after. The 2007 cycle in the full-sample HP estimate looks substantially more positive than it did in real time: perhaps \(+3\) to \(+4\%\). The economy was running considerably hotter than the real-time filter suggested.


Show code вЂ” HP end-point bias
cutoff = "2007-10-01"   # data through 2007 Q4

# Real-time HP: estimated on data up to cutoff only
log_sa_rt          = log_sa_full[log_sa_full.index <= cutoff]
cycle_hp_rt, _     = hpfilter(log_sa_rt, lamb=1600)

# Full-sample HP (already computed)
cycle_hp_full      = cycle_hp   # from the block above

# Restrict full-sample to the real-time window for overlay
cycle_hp_full_sub  = cycle_hp_full[cycle_hp_full.index <= cutoff]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 3.5))

ax.plot(cycle_hp_rt.index,       cycle_hp_rt.values * 100,
        color=EO_COPPER,  lw=1.1, label="Real-time HP (data through 2007 Q4)")
ax.plot(cycle_hp_full_sub.index, cycle_hp_full_sub.values * 100,
        color=EO_SKYBLUE, lw=1.1, ls="--", label="Full-sample HP (data through 2024)")
ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, lw=0.6, ls=":")
ax.fill_between(cycle_hp_rt.index,
                cycle_hp_rt.values * 100,
                cycle_hp_full_sub.values * 100,
                alpha=0.15, color=EO_LAVENDER, label="Revision gap")
shade_recessions(ax, start=PLOT_START, end="2010-01-01")
ax.set_title("Real-Time vs. Full-Sample HP Cycle вЂ” Pre-Crisis Period")
ax.set_ylabel("% deviation from trend")
ax.legend(loc="lower left", fontsize=6)
eo_style_ax(ax)
eo_suptitle(fig, "HP Filter End-Point Bias: Real-Time vs. Full-Sample, 1947вЂ“2007")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








FigureВ 2.5: HP filter end-point bias: real-time vs.В full-sample cycle estimates for log SA real GDP. The copper line shows the HP cycle computed using only data available through 2007 Q4 (the last pre-crisis quarter). The blue line shows the HP cycle computed on the full sample through 2024. Both use О»=1600. In the years immediately preceding the 2008 recession (shaded), the real-time HP cycle is substantially smaller вЂ” and in some quarters near zero вЂ” while the full-sample estimate shows a clear positive boom. A policymaker relying on the real-time HP cycle in 2006вЂ“2007 would have seen a roughly neutral output gap; the same data, re-evaluated with hindsight, reveals a significant overheating. The gap between the two lines near 2007 is the end-point bias.





The lavender band in the figure is the revision gap вЂ” the difference between what a policymaker would have seen in real time and what the same method says in hindsight. In the quarters immediately before the 2008 recession, the gap is substantial and in the wrong direction: the real-time HP cycle understates the boom, which means it also understates the vulnerability of an economy running above potential. A central bank tightening policy in response to the real-time cycle would have seen a reason to tighten modestly; with hindsight, the case for tightening was considerably stronger.
This is the policy consequence of end-point bias: systematic errors in real-time cycle estimates, concentrated exactly when the estimate matters most вЂ” at the frontier of the data. The Hamilton filter, by construction, does not have this property. Its cycle estimate at any date \(t\) depends only on observations available well before \(t\) (since the residual is dated \(t+h\) and uses data from \(t\) backward), so the real-time and full-sample estimates coincide.
The business cycle, in other words, is real вЂ” the economy genuinely expands and contracts вЂ” but its precise shape and magnitude are decomposition-dependent. When you read that the output gap is currently \(+1.5\%\) or \(-2\%\), you are reading a number that depends heavily on choices that rarely appear in the headline.
crumbs: 2В  Chapter 2: Decomposition, Smoothing, and Business Cycle Analysis

title: 2В  Chapter 2: Decomposition, Smoothing, and Business Cycle Analysis
text:
2.4 Seasonal Adjustment in Practice

Why Seasonal Adjustment Matters
Seasonal patterns in economic data are large, predictable, and economically uninteresting вЂ” which is exactly what makes them dangerous. A policymaker looking at raw retail sales in January would see a sharp drop from December every single year, regardless of underlying economic conditions. A central bank reacting to every seasonal swing in the unemployment rate would be setting monetary policy in response to noise. Seasonal adjustment strips out these predictable rhythms so that the signal вЂ” the underlying trend and cycle вЂ” is easier to see.
Most official economic statistics are reported in seasonally adjusted form as the default. The employment situation report, the GDP advance estimate, industrial production, new housing starts вЂ” all are seasonally adjusted before publication. The methodology behind those adjustments matters because it shapes the data that researchers, forecasters, and policymakers use every day.


X-13-ARIMA-SEATS: The GovernmentвЂ™s Method
The US Bureau of Economic Analysis, Bureau of Labor Statistics, and statistical agencies across most developed economies adjust their data using X-13-ARIMA-SEATS, a software package maintained by the US Census Bureau. Understanding what it does вЂ” even without running it вЂ” is essential for interpreting official statistics.
X-13 is the latest in a long lineage of X-11 programs developed at the Census Bureau since the 1950s. It combines two conceptually distinct approaches:
The X-11 component is a sophisticated iterative moving-average filter. Starting from a basic trend-cycle estimate, it extracts seasonal factors, removes them, re-estimates the trend on the adjusted series, re-estimates the seasonal factors, and iterates until convergence. The key advancement over classical decomposition is that it applies different moving averages to different parts of the series, allows seasonal factors to evolve slowly over time, and includes outlier detection and correction. This flexibility is why official seasonal adjustments are smoother and more credible than simple classical decomposition.
The ARIMA component addresses a limitation of all filter-based methods: they lose observations at the ends of the sample, precisely where the most recent and policy-relevant data sit. X-13 pre-extends the series by fitting an ARIMA model (covered in Chapter 3) and forecasting several periods ahead, then applies the moving-average filter to the extended series, and finally discards the forecasted values. This eliminates the end-point problem that plagues classical methods.
The SEATS component (Signal Extraction in ARIMA Time Series) is an alternative signal-extraction procedure based on model-based decomposition: it fits a SARIMA model to the observed series, then uses the estimated model to decompose the spectrum of the series into trend, seasonal, and irregular components. SEATS produces smoother seasonal factors than X-11 under some conditions and has a firmer statistical foundation.
Why does X-13 require a dedicated binary? Because it is a complex Fortran program with decades of accumulated logic for handling calendars (trading day effects, holiday effects, leap years), performing outlier detection, selecting ARIMA specifications, and producing diagnostic statistics. It is not a simple Python function вЂ” it is an industrial-grade statistical production system. The statsmodels library provides a Python wrapper (statsmodels.tsa.x13.x13_arima_analysis) that calls the X-13 binary, but the binary itself must be installed separately from the Census Bureau. For most analytical purposes вЂ” and for all the work in this course вЂ” the methods we can implement directly in Python are sufficient and considerably more transparent.


STL Decomposition: A Flexible, Portable Alternative
The method we will use for seasonal decomposition is STL вЂ” Seasonal-Trend decomposition using Loess вЂ” introduced by Cleveland et al.В (1990). STL is implemented natively in statsmodels without any external binary dependency, it is highly flexible, and it handles many of the practical challenges that classical decomposition cannot.
STL is an iterative procedure. It alternates between two passes: an inner loop that updates the seasonal and trend estimates given the current decomposition, and an outer loop that computes robustness weights to reduce the influence of outliers. The trend at each step is estimated using Loess (locally weighted polynomial regression), which fits a low-degree polynomial to a local neighborhood of the data at each point вЂ” more flexible than a global moving average and less prone to distortion near structural shifts.
The key user-controlled parameters are: - period: the seasonal period (\(m = 4\) for quarterly, \(12\) for monthly) - seasonal: the width of the seasonal Loess window (larger = smoother seasonal factors) - trend: the width of the trend Loess window (larger = smoother trend) - robust: whether to use the outlier-resistant outer loop
The main differences from X-13 are worth stating clearly. STL treats the seasonal component as slowly varying but does not model calendar effects (trading day counts, Easter, holidays). X-13 explicitly adjusts for these. STL is additive by default (or multiplicative on logs). X-13 integrates ARIMA-based forecasting to avoid end-point loss. STL is fully transparent and reproduced in Python; X-13 is an institutional production standard with decades of accumulated refinements. For research and teaching, STL is the right tool. For producing the official figures that appear in a statistical release, X-13 is the standard.


Show code вЂ” STL decomposition
stl    = STL(gdp["Log NSA"], period=4, seasonal=7, robust=True)
result = stl.fit()

fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(6, 6), sharex=True)

# Panel 1: observed + trend overlaid
ax = axes[0]
ax.plot(gdp.index, gdp["Log NSA"],   color=EO_CHARCOAL, lw=0.9, label="Observed")
ax.plot(result.trend.index, result.trend.values, color=EO_COPPER, lw=1.2, ls="--", label="STL Trend (Loess)")
shade_recessions(ax, start=PLOT_START)
ax.set_title("Observed and Trend")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=6)
eo_style_ax(ax)

# Panel 2: seasonal
ax = axes[1]
ax.plot(result.seasonal.index, result.seasonal.values, color=EO_SKYBLUE, lw=0.9)
ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, lw=0.5, ls=":")
shade_recessions(ax, start=PLOT_START)
ax.set_title("Seasonal")
eo_style_ax(ax)

# Panel 3: irregular
ax = axes[2]
ax.plot(result.resid.index, result.resid.values, color=EO_TERRACOTTA, lw=0.9)
ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, lw=0.5, ls=":")
ax.fill_between(result.resid.index, result.resid.values, 0,
                where=(result.resid.values < 0), color=EO_TERRACOTTA, alpha=0.2)
shade_recessions(ax, start=PLOT_START)
ax.set_title("Irregular (Remainder)")
axes[-1].set_xlabel("")
eo_style_ax(ax)

end_yr = gdp.index[-1].year
eo_suptitle(fig, f"STL Decomposition вЂ” Log NSA Real GDP, {gdp.index[0].year}вЂ“{end_yr}")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








FigureВ 2.6: STL decomposition of log NSA real GDP. Top panel: observed log level overlaid with the STL Loess trend вЂ” the trend is smoother and more flexible than the classical moving average, adapting to the productivity slowdown of the 1970s and the post-2008 recovery. Middle panel: seasonal component, allowed to evolve slowly over time. Bottom panel: irregular remainder, the component that contains the business cycle signal.







Show code вЂ” STL vs official SA comparison
# STL seasonal-adjusted = observed - seasonal component (in logs)
gdp["Log STL-SA"] = gdp["Log NSA"] - result.seasonal

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 3))
ax.plot(gdp.index, gdp["Log SA"],
        color=EO_SKYBLUE, lw=1.2, label="Official SA (GDPC1)")
ax.plot(gdp.index, gdp["Log STL-SA"],
        color=EO_COPPER, lw=0.9, ls="--", label="STL-adjusted (our estimate)")
shade_recessions(ax, start=PLOT_START)
ax.set_title("Log Real GDP: Official vs. STL-Adjusted")
ax.set_ylabel("Log (2017 USD, SAAR)")
ax.legend(loc="upper left")
eo_style_ax(ax)
end_yr = gdp.index[-1].year
eo_suptitle(fig, f"STL Seasonal Adjustment vs. Official GDPC1, {gdp.index[0].year}вЂ“{end_yr}")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








FigureВ 2.7: Comparison of STL-adjusted and officially SA real GDP. The STL adjustment (copper dashed) closely tracks the BEAвЂ™s X-13-based SA series (blue), with the two differing primarily in periods of structural change (post-2008, post-2020) where X-13вЂ™s ARIMA-based end-extension and calendar adjustment produce marginally smoother estimates. The correspondence is close enough for research and teaching purposes.





The correspondence between the STL-adjusted series and the official SA series is close вЂ” close enough to validate the approach for our purposes. The differences are largest in the most recent quarters and around the major structural breaks (2008вЂ“09, 2020), exactly where X-13вЂ™s ARIMA pre-extension and calendar adjustment provide the most additional precision.


What Seasonal Adjustment Removes вЂ” and What It Does Not
It is worth being precise about what seasonal adjustment actually does to the data. It removes the component of variation that is systematic and calendar-driven вЂ” the predictable swings associated with the time of year. It does not remove all short-run volatility; the irregular component survives. It does not smooth out recessions; a recession that hits hardest in Q4 will still appear as a sharp decline in the adjusted series, even if Q4 is normally a strong quarter.
One practical implication: seasonal adjustment can interact with structural change in ways that produce artifacts. If the 2020 pandemic caused an unprecedented collapse in Q2 вЂ” far outside the range of historical Q2 observations вЂ” then seasonal adjustment algorithms estimated on pre-pandemic data will temporarily misestimate the seasonal factor for Q2 in subsequent years. This is why the BLS and BEA issued multiple revisions to seasonal factors in 2020вЂ“2022 and why analysts following real-time data always need to be aware of the vintage of seasonal factors being applied.
crumbs: 2В  Chapter 2: Decomposition, Smoothing, and Business Cycle Analysis

title: 2В  Chapter 2: Decomposition, Smoothing, and Business Cycle Analysis
text:
2.5 Exponential Smoothing

A Different Approach: Adapting to New Information
The decomposition methods of the previous sections were retrospective: given the full series, extract components by filtering or regression. Forecasting with those components is straightforward вЂ” extrapolate the trend forward, add the seasonal factors, assume the irregular averages out вЂ” but the method itself was not designed with forecasting as its primary purpose.
Exponential smoothing takes the forecasting problem as its starting point. The question it answers is: given a stream of observations arriving one at a time, how should we update our estimate of the current level of the series as each new observation arrives?
The insight is that recent observations should count for more than old ones. An observation from last quarter is more informative about where the economy is right now than an observation from ten years ago. But the observation from ten years ago should not be thrown away entirely вЂ” it contains some information, just less. Exponential smoothing formalizes this intuition by assigning geometrically declining weights to past observations.


Simple Exponential Smoothing
Simple exponential smoothing (SES) is designed for series with no trend and no seasonality вЂ” a level that fluctuates around a slowly moving mean. The smoothed level at time \(t\) is:
\[\hat{y}_{t+1|t} = \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)\hat{y}_{t|t-1} \tag{2.5}\]
where \(\alpha \in (0,1)\) is the smoothing parameter. The forecast for the next period is a weighted blend of the current observation \(y_t\) and last periodвЂ™s forecast \(\hat{y}_{t|t-1}\).
Expanding the recursion reveals why this is called exponential smoothing:
\[\hat{y}_{t+1|t} = \alpha y_t + \alpha(1-\alpha)y_{t-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)^2 y_{t-2} + \cdots\]
The weight on observation \(y_{t-k}\) is \(\alpha(1-\alpha)^k\), which declines geometrically in \(k\). A small \(\alpha\) (close to zero) places heavy weight on old observations: the smoothed level changes slowly and the forecast is nearly a long-run average. A large \(\alpha\) (close to one) places almost all weight on the most recent observation: the forecast tracks the data closely and reacts quickly to new information.






NoteDefinition 2.3 вЂ” Simple Exponential Smoothing



Given observations \(y_1, \ldots, y_T\) and a smoothing parameter \(\alpha \in (0,1)\), the SES forecast is:
\[\hat{y}_{t+1|t} = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)\hat{y}_{t|t-1}, \quad t = 1, 2, \ldots, T\]
initialized at \(\hat{y}_{1|0} = y_1\) (or estimated as a free parameter). The \(h\)-step-ahead forecast is flat: \(\hat{y}_{T+h|T} = \hat{y}_{T+1|T}\) for all \(h \geq 1\). SES is appropriate for series with no trend and no seasonality.


An equivalent and illuminating way to write equation (2.5) is the error-correction form:
\[\hat{y}_{t+1|t} = \hat{y}_{t|t-1} + \alpha e_t, \qquad e_t = y_t - \hat{y}_{t|t-1}\]
Each new forecast adjusts the previous forecast by a fraction \(\alpha\) of the most recent forecast error. This makes the adaptive learning interpretation explicit: after each observation, we revise our estimate of the level by a fraction \(\alpha\) of how wrong we were. Large \(\alpha\) means aggressive updating; small \(\alpha\) means conservative updating.
SES produces flat multi-step forecasts: since there is no trend in the model, the best forecast at any horizon \(h \geq 1\) is the current smoothed level. This is appropriate for stationary series, but for trending series like GDP we need to extend the framework.


HoltвЂ™s Method: Adding a Trend
HoltвЂ™s linear exponential smoothing extends SES by maintaining a separate smoothed estimate of the trend alongside the level. The equations are:
\[\hat{\ell}_t = \alpha y_t + (1-\alpha)(\hat{\ell}_{t-1} + \hat{b}_{t-1}) \tag{2.6}\]
\[\hat{b}_t = \beta(\hat{\ell}_t - \hat{\ell}_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)\hat{b}_{t-1} \tag{2.7}\]
\[\hat{y}_{t+h|t} = \hat{\ell}_t + h\hat{b}_t \tag{2.8}\]
Here \(\hat{\ell}_t\) is the smoothed level and \(\hat{b}_t\) is the smoothed trend (slope). Equation (2.6) updates the level as a blend of the new observation \(y_t\) and a one-step-ahead forecast from the previous level-plus-trend. Equation (2.7) updates the trend as a blend of the current change in level \(\hat{\ell}_t - \hat{\ell}_{t-1}\) and the previous trend estimate. The \(h\)-step-ahead forecast (2.8) projects the current level forward at the current slope.
Two smoothing parameters now need to be chosen: \(\alpha\) controls how quickly the level adapts, and \(\beta\) controls how quickly the trend adapts. Both lie in \((0,1)\). A small \(\beta\) means the estimated trend changes slowly вЂ” the model assumes the trend is persistent. A large \(\beta\) means the trend adapts quickly to recent changes in slope.
An important variant is HoltвЂ™s damped trend method, which multiplies the trend by a damping factor \(\phi \in (0,1)\) at each forecast horizon:
\[\hat{y}_{t+h|t} = \hat{\ell}_t + (\phi + \phi^2 + \cdots + \phi^h)\hat{b}_t\]
For \(h = 1\) this is the same as the undamped Holt forecast. For large \(h\), the cumulative factor \(\sum_{i=1}^h \phi^i \to \phi/(1-\phi)\) as \(h \to \infty\), so the long-run forecast approaches a finite level rather than growing without bound. Empirical evidence consistently shows that damped trend forecasts outperform undamped ones at medium and long horizons: in practice, trends rarely persist at their recent estimated slope for many periods ahead, and damping provides insurance against overextrapolation.


Show code вЂ” SES and HoltвЂ™s method
series_fit = gdp["Log STL-SA"].dropna()

# SES with two alpha values for illustration
ses_low  = SimpleExpSmoothing(series_fit).fit(smoothing_level=0.05, optimized=False)
ses_high = SimpleExpSmoothing(series_fit).fit(smoothing_level=0.4,  optimized=False)

# Holt (undamped and damped)
holt_und  = Holt(series_fit).fit(optimized=True)
holt_dmp  = Holt(series_fit, damped_trend=True).fit(optimized=True)

h_fcast   = 12   # forecast horizon

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(6, 5), sharex=False)

# в”Ђв”Ђ SES panel в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
ax = axes[0]
ses_start = "2000-01-01"
s = series_fit[series_fit.index >= ses_start]
ax.plot(s.index, s.values, color=EO_CHARCOAL, lw=0.9, label="Observed")
ax.plot(s.index, ses_low.fittedvalues[ses_low.fittedvalues.index >= ses_start],
        color=EO_COPPER, lw=1.0, ls="--", label=f"SES О±=0.05")
ax.plot(s.index, ses_high.fittedvalues[ses_high.fittedvalues.index >= ses_start],
        color=EO_SKYBLUE, lw=1.0, ls="--", label=f"SES О±=0.40")
shade_recessions(ax, start=ses_start)
ax.set_title("Simple Exponential Smoothing (2000вЂ“present)")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=6)
eo_style_ax(ax)

# в”Ђв”Ђ Holt panel в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
ax = axes[1]
fcast_und = holt_und.forecast(h_fcast)
fcast_dmp = holt_dmp.forecast(h_fcast)

last_date  = series_fit.index[-1]
fcast_idx  = pd.date_range(last_date, periods=h_fcast + 1, freq="QS")[1:]

ax.plot(s.index, s.values, color=EO_CHARCOAL, lw=0.9, label="Observed")
ax.plot(s.index,
        holt_und.fittedvalues[holt_und.fittedvalues.index >= ses_start],
        color=EO_SKYBLUE, lw=0.9, ls="--", label=f"Holt undamped (О±={holt_und.params['smoothing_level']:.2f}, ОІ={holt_und.params['smoothing_trend']:.2f})")
ax.plot(s.index,
        holt_dmp.fittedvalues[holt_dmp.fittedvalues.index >= ses_start],
        color=EO_TERRACOTTA, lw=0.9, ls="--", label=f"Holt damped (П†={holt_dmp.params['damping_trend']:.2f})")
ax.plot(fcast_idx, fcast_und.values, color=EO_SKYBLUE,    lw=1.1, ls="-")
ax.plot(fcast_idx, fcast_dmp.values, color=EO_TERRACOTTA, lw=1.1, ls="-")
ax.axvline(last_date, color=EO_CHARCOAL, lw=0.6, ls=":")
shade_recessions(ax, start=ses_start)
ax.set_title("Holt's Method: Undamped and Damped Trend (2000вЂ“present)")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=6)
eo_style_ax(ax)

eo_suptitle(fig, "Exponential Smoothing вЂ” Log STL-SA Real GDP")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








FigureВ 2.8: Simple exponential smoothing (top) and HoltвЂ™s method with and without damping (bottom), applied to the log STL-SA real GDP series. SES with small О± produces a smooth level that lags the data considerably; larger О± tracks the data closely but produces a jagged level. HoltвЂ™s method with trend produces forecasts that extrapolate the recent slope linearly (blue) or with damping (terracotta), which flattens out at longer horizons. Parameters are estimated by maximum likelihood.





The figure illustrates the key behavioral difference between the two methods. SES produces a smooth level that works well for series without trend, but it systematically lags a trending series вЂ” it is always trying to catch up. HoltвЂ™s method follows the trend by construction. The undamped forecast extrapolates the recent slope linearly and diverges from the damped version over longer horizons. For a series as persistently trending as GDP, the damped version is almost always more appropriate: it is skeptical that the most recently estimated growth rate will persist indefinitely.
crumbs: 2В  Chapter 2: Decomposition, Smoothing, and Business Cycle Analysis

title: 2В  Chapter 2: Decomposition, Smoothing, and Business Cycle Analysis
text:
2.6 The ETS Framework

Error, Trend, and Seasonality
The exponential smoothing methods developed so far can be organized into a unified framework known as ETS вЂ” named for its three components: Error, Trend, and Seasonality. This framework, developed formally by Hyndman et al.В (2002, 2008), achieves two things. It provides a systematic taxonomy of all sensible combinations of exponential smoothing components, and it provides a formal statistical model вЂ” with likelihood function and prediction intervals вЂ” for each combination. This is what allows principled parameter estimation and proper uncertainty quantification.
Each of the three components can take different forms:

Error (E): additive (A) or multiplicative (M)
Trend (T): none (N), additive (A), additive damped (Ad), multiplicative (M), multiplicative damped (Md)
Seasonality (S): none (N), additive (A), multiplicative (M)

The notation ETS(E,T,S) identifies a specific model. ETS(A,N,N), for example, is simple exponential smoothing with additive errors вЂ” the SES model of Section 2.5. ETS(A,A,N) is HoltвЂ™s undamped linear method. ETS(A,Ad,N) is HoltвЂ™s damped method. The Holt-Winters seasonal models are ETS(A,A,A) and ETS(A,A,M).


Key ETS Models
The four cases that matter most in macroeconomic applications are:
ETS(A,N,N) вЂ” Simple Exponential Smoothing. No trend, no seasonality, additive error. The level equation is \(\hat{\ell}_t = \hat{\ell}_{t-1} + \alpha e_t\) where \(e_t = y_t - \hat{\ell}_{t-1}\). Forecasts are flat. Appropriate for stationary or slowly-drifting series with no seasonal pattern.
ETS(A,A,N) вЂ” HoltвЂ™s Linear Method. Additive trend, no seasonality, additive error. Level and slope both updated each period. Forecasts follow a linear projection from the current state. Appropriate for trending non-seasonal series. The undamped version extrapolates linearly; caution is warranted at long horizons.
ETS(A,Ad,N) вЂ” HoltвЂ™s Damped Method. Additive damped trend, no seasonality. Forecasts converge to a finite level as \(h \to \infty\). Empirically, this is often the single best-performing exponential smoothing model across a wide range of real datasets, particularly at medium horizons.
ETS(A,A,A) вЂ” Holt-Winters Additive. Additive trend, additive seasonal, additive error. Level, trend, and seasonal factor are all updated each period. The additive seasonal component assumes the seasonal swings are constant in absolute size. Appropriate when the series has been log-transformed (so that multiplicative seasonality in levels becomes additive seasonality in logs).






NoteDefinition 2.4 вЂ” The Holt-Winters Additive Model: ETS(A,A,A)



Given a seasonal period \(m\), the state equations are:
\[\hat{\ell}_t = \alpha(y_t - \hat{s}_{t-m}) + (1-\alpha)(\hat{\ell}_{t-1} + \hat{b}_{t-1}) \tag{2.9}\]
\[\hat{b}_t = \beta(\hat{\ell}_t - \hat{\ell}_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)\hat{b}_{t-1} \tag{2.10}\]
\[\hat{s}_t = \gamma(y_t - \hat{\ell}_{t-1} - \hat{b}_{t-1}) + (1-\gamma)\hat{s}_{t-m} \tag{2.11}\]
\[\hat{y}_{t+h|t} = \hat{\ell}_t + h\hat{b}_t + \hat{s}_{t+h-m(k+1)} \tag{2.12}\]
where \(k = \lfloor (h-1)/m \rfloor\) ensures the correct seasonal index is used, and \(\gamma \in (0, 1-\alpha)\) is the seasonal smoothing parameter. Three smoothing parameters вЂ” \(\alpha\), \(\beta\), \(\gamma\) вЂ” and the initial states \(\{\hat{\ell}_0, \hat{b}_0, \hat{s}_{1-m}, \ldots, \hat{s}_0\}\) are estimated jointly by maximum likelihood.


The three smoothing equations have a parallel structure. The level (2.9) is updated as a blend of the de-seasonalized observation \(y_t - \hat{s}_{t-m}\) and the extrapolated level from the previous state. The trend (2.10) updates the slope. The seasonal factor (2.11) updates the seasonal index for period \(t\) as a blend of the de-trended observation and the same-season factor from one full year ago. The forecast (2.12) adds the projected level, the projected trend, and the appropriate seasonal factor.


The Full ETS Taxonomy
Not all 30 possible combinations of (E, T, S) components are numerically stable or practically useful. The table below shows the complete taxonomy of valid models.

Models with multiplicative errors (M,В·,В·) have explicit likelihood functions only when all observations are positive. Models with (M,A,В·) or (M,A,M) components can exhibit numerical instability and are sometimes excluded in automatic selection procedures. In practice, models with additive errors and either additive or no seasonality cover the vast majority of macroeconomic applications.



For our log GDP series, the relevant candidates are models with additive errors and additive seasonality: (A,N,A), (A,A,A), and (A,Ad,A). Since we are working on log-transformed data (converting multiplicative seasonality in levels to additive seasonality in logs), we do not need the multiplicative seasonal variants.


Parameter Estimation: Maximum Likelihood
Parameters in ETS models вЂ” the smoothing parameters \(\alpha\), \(\beta\), \(\gamma\), \(\phi\) and the initial states вЂ” are estimated by maximum likelihood. Given a model specification, the one-step-ahead forecast errors \(e_t = y_t - \hat{y}_{t|t-1}\) are computed recursively. Under the assumption of Gaussian errors, the log-likelihood is:
\[\ell(\theta) = -\frac{T}{2}\log(2\pi) - \frac{T}{2}\log\hat{\sigma}^2 - \frac{1}{2\hat{\sigma}^2}\sum_{t=1}^T e_t^2\]
where \(\hat{\sigma}^2 = T^{-1}\sum e_t^2\) is the variance of the one-step errors. Maximizing this is equivalent to minimizing the sum of squared one-step errors вЂ” which is also what we would get from least squares. The difference with full MLE is that the initial states are estimated as free parameters rather than set by convention, and that the likelihood framework extends naturally to prediction intervals and model comparison via AIC.
statsmodels implements this through ExponentialSmoothing, which accepts a model specification and returns fitted parameters, smoothed states, and methods for forecasting and simulation.
crumbs: 2В  Chapter 2: Decomposition, Smoothing, and Business Cycle Analysis

title: 2В  Chapter 2: Decomposition, Smoothing, and Business Cycle Analysis
text:
2.7 Forecasting with ETS

Point Forecasts
With parameters estimated, generating point forecasts from an ETS model is a matter of running the state equations forward from the end of the sample. For ETS(A,Ad,A), the \(h\)-step-ahead forecast is:
\[\hat{y}_{T+h|T} = \hat{\ell}_T + \left(\sum_{i=1}^{h}\phi^i\right)\hat{b}_T + \hat{s}_{T+h-m(k+1)}\]
The first term is the current level. The second is the cumulative trend, damped by successive powers of \(\phi\). The third adds back the appropriate seasonal factor from the end of the fitted sample.
For a log-transformed series, the forecasts are in log units. Converting back to levels requires care: because \(\exp(\hat{y} + \hat{\sigma}^2/2) > \exp(\hat{y})\) for any positive \(\hat{\sigma}^2\), the unbiased point forecast in levels is not simply \(\exp(\hat{y}_{T+h|T})\) вЂ” a bias correction of \(\exp(\hat{\sigma}^2_h/2)\) is needed, where \(\hat{\sigma}^2_h\) is the \(h\)-step forecast error variance.


Prediction Intervals
A key advantage of the ETS framework over ad hoc smoothing is that it provides principled prediction intervals. Under the additive error specification, the \(h\)-step forecast error variance can be computed analytically for each model. For ETS(A,N,N):
\[\sigma^2_h = \sigma^2[1 + \alpha^2(h-1)]\]
which grows with the forecast horizon \(h\) вЂ” appropriately capturing the fact that uncertainty compounds as we look further ahead. More complex ETS models have correspondingly more complex expressions for \(\sigma^2_h\), but all share the property that intervals widen with horizon.
Prediction intervals are not confidence intervals. A 95% prediction interval for \(y_{T+h}\) says: if the model is correctly specified and parameters are known, 95% of future realizations will fall within this band. It accounts for forecast uncertainty вЂ” the randomness of future innovations вЂ” but not for parameter uncertainty or model uncertainty. In practice, intervals computed from estimated parameters will be slightly too narrow. Chapter 5 returns to this issue when we discuss the empirical coverage of prediction intervals and methods for calibrating them.


A Holt-Winters Forecast for NSA GDP
We now fit the full Holt-Winters additive model to the log NSA GDP series and generate forecasts eight quarters ahead вЂ” two years beyond the end of the sample.


Show code вЂ” Holt-Winters forecast
series_nsa = gdp["Log NSA"].dropna()

# Fit ETS(A,Ad,A): additive error, additive damped trend, additive seasonal
hw_model = ExponentialSmoothing(
    series_nsa,
    trend="add",
    damped_trend=True,
    seasonal="add",
    seasonal_periods=4,
    initialization_method="estimated",
).fit(optimized=True)

h_ahead  = 8
fcast    = hw_model.forecast(h_ahead)

# Simulation-based prediction intervals
sim      = hw_model.simulate(h_ahead, repetitions=5000, error="add")
pi_80_lo = np.percentile(sim, 10, axis=1)
pi_80_hi = np.percentile(sim, 90, axis=1)
pi_95_lo = np.percentile(sim, 2.5, axis=1)
pi_95_hi = np.percentile(sim, 97.5, axis=1)

fcast_idx  = fcast.index
hw_start   = "2000-01-01"

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(6, 5))

# в”Ђв”Ђ Main forecast panel в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
ax = axes[0]
obs_plot = series_nsa[series_nsa.index >= hw_start]
ax.plot(obs_plot.index, obs_plot.values, color=EO_CHARCOAL, lw=0.9, label="Observed")
ax.plot(hw_model.fittedvalues[hw_model.fittedvalues.index >= hw_start].index,
        hw_model.fittedvalues[hw_model.fittedvalues.index >= hw_start].values,
        color=EO_COPPER, lw=0.8, ls="--", label="Fitted (in-sample)")
ax.plot(fcast_idx, fcast.values, color=EO_SKYBLUE, lw=1.2, label="Forecast")
ax.fill_between(fcast_idx, pi_80_lo, pi_80_hi,
                color=EO_SKYBLUE, alpha=0.25, label="80% PI")
ax.fill_between(fcast_idx, pi_95_lo, pi_95_hi,
                color=EO_SKYBLUE, alpha=0.12, label="95% PI")
ax.axvline(series_nsa.index[-1], color=EO_CHARCOAL, lw=0.6, ls=":")
shade_recessions(ax, start=hw_start)
ax.set_title("ETS(A,Ad,A) Forecast вЂ” Log NSA Real GDP")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=5.5, ncol=2)
eo_style_ax(ax)

# в”Ђв”Ђ Residual panel в”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђв”Ђ
ax = axes[1]
resid_plot = hw_model.resid[hw_model.resid.index >= hw_start]
ax.plot(resid_plot.index, resid_plot.values, color=EO_TERRACOTTA, lw=0.8)
ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, lw=0.6, ls=":")
shade_recessions(ax, start=hw_start)
ax.set_title("In-Sample Residuals")
ax.set_ylabel("Residual (log units)")
eo_style_ax(ax)

# Print parameter summary
print(f"Fitted parameters:")
print(f"  О± (level):   {hw_model.params['smoothing_level']:.4f}")
print(f"  ОІ (trend):   {hw_model.params['smoothing_trend']:.4f}")
print(f"  Оі (seasonal):{hw_model.params['smoothing_seasonal']:.4f}")
print(f"  П† (damping): {hw_model.params['damping_trend']:.4f}")
print(f"  AIC:         {hw_model.aic:.2f}")
print(f"  BIC:         {hw_model.bic:.2f}")

end_yr = fcast_idx[-1].year
eo_suptitle(fig, f"Holt-Winters Forecast вЂ” Log NSA Real GDP, 2000вЂ“{end_yr}")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()


Fitted parameters:
  О± (level):   0.8536
  ОІ (trend):   0.0000
  Оі (seasonal):0.0000
  П† (damping): 0.9950
  AIC:         -776.11
  BIC:         -753.51








FigureВ 2.9: Holt-Winters ETS(A,Ad,A) forecast for log NSA real GDP, 2000вЂ“2026. The model is fit on data through 2024 Q3 and forecasts eight quarters ahead. The seasonal pattern is extrapolated from the estimated seasonal factors; the trend is damped, reflecting appropriate skepticism about the persistence of the recent growth rate. The 80% and 95% prediction intervals (shaded bands) widen with horizon. NBER recessions are shaded; the sharp 2020 outlier is visible in both the data and the residual diagnostics.





Several features of the fitted model are worth noting. The level smoothing parameter \(\alpha\) reflects how quickly the model updates its estimate of the current level in response to new observations. The damping parameter \(\phi\) (close to 1 but below it) indicates modest damping вЂ” the model retains most of the trend momentum but expects it to fade somewhat over the forecast horizon. The seasonal parameter \(\gamma\) controls how much the model learns about seasonal factors from each new year of data; a small value means the estimated seasonal pattern changes slowly, which is appropriate for a series whose seasonal structure has been stable for decades.
The residual panel deserves attention. Well-behaved residuals from an ETS model should look like white noise вЂ” random, with no obvious patterns, no remaining seasonal structure, and no persistent runs above or below zero. The 2020 Q2 observation produces a large negative outlier that the model cannot anticipate: this is COVID-19, an exogenous shock outside the information set of any statistical model. Similarly, the sharp V-shaped recovery in 2020 Q3 produces a large positive residual. Both are reflections of the modelвЂ™s appropriate ignorance about regime-changing events, not evidence of misspecification.


A Simple Train-Test Exercise
A Holt-Winters forecast that perfectly fits in-sample tells us very little about how useful the model is. To evaluate performance honestly, we should hold out some data, fit the model on the training set, and examine how well the forecasts track the held-out test set. We do this here as a first illustration; the full framework for forecast evaluation вЂ” including proper scoring rules, the Diebold-Mariano test, and forecast combination вЂ” is developed in Chapter 5.


Show code вЂ” Train-test split
train = series_nsa[series_nsa.index < "2020-01-01"]
test  = series_nsa[series_nsa.index >= "2020-01-01"]

hw_train = ExponentialSmoothing(
    train,
    trend="add",
    damped_trend=True,
    seasonal="add",
    seasonal_periods=4,
    initialization_method="estimated",
).fit(optimized=True)

h_test    = len(test)
fcast_tt  = hw_train.forecast(h_test)
sim_tt    = hw_train.simulate(h_test, repetitions=5000, error="add")
pi80_lo   = np.percentile(sim_tt, 10, axis=1)
pi80_hi   = np.percentile(sim_tt, 90, axis=1)
pi95_lo   = np.percentile(sim_tt, 2.5, axis=1)
pi95_hi   = np.percentile(sim_tt, 97.5, axis=1)

rmse = np.sqrt(np.mean((fcast_tt.values - test.values)**2))

# Show last 10 years of training data for context
train_window = train[train.index >= (train.index[-1] - pd.DateOffset(years=10))]
train_yr     = train.index[0].year
test_end_yr  = test.index[-1].year

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 4))
ax.plot(train_window.index, train_window.values,
        color=EO_CHARCOAL, lw=0.9, label="Training data (last 10 yrs shown)")
ax.plot(test.index, test.values, color=EO_CHARCOAL, lw=0.9, ls="--", label="Test data (actual)")
ax.plot(fcast_tt.index, fcast_tt.values, color=EO_COPPER, lw=1.2, label="Forecast")
ax.fill_between(fcast_tt.index, pi80_lo, pi80_hi,
                color=EO_COPPER, alpha=0.25, label="80% PI")
ax.fill_between(fcast_tt.index, pi95_lo, pi95_hi,
                color=EO_COPPER, alpha=0.12, label="95% PI")
ax.axvline(train.index[-1], color=EO_CHARCOAL, lw=0.6, ls=":")
ax.text(test.index[1], ax.get_ylim()[0] + 0.01,
        f"RMSE = {rmse:.4f}", fontsize=6, color=EO_CHARCOAL)
ax.set_title(f"Train-Test: Fit on {train_yr}вЂ“{train.index[-1].year}, Forecast {test.index[0].year}вЂ“{test_end_yr}")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=5.5, ncol=2)
eo_style_ax(ax)
eo_suptitle(fig, "Holt-Winters Out-of-Sample Forecast вЂ” Log NSA Real GDP")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








FigureВ 2.10: Train-test evaluation of the Holt-Winters ETS(A,Ad,A) model on log NSA real GDP. The model is fit on 1947вЂ“2019 Q4 (training set, 292 observations) and forecasts 20 quarters ahead into 2020вЂ“2024 (test set). The 2020 COVID shock falls squarely in the test period and produces large errors in 2020 Q1вЂ“Q2. Abstracting from the pandemic quarters, the model tracks the subsequent recovery and the 2023вЂ“2024 level reasonably well, with point forecasts staying within the 80% prediction interval for most of the test period.





The train-test exercise illustrates a lesson that comes up repeatedly in forecasting: a modelвЂ™s out-of-sample performance during a period containing an extreme event tells us more about the event than about the model. The 2020 collapse and recovery are simply outside the support of anything a time series model estimated on postwar data could anticipate. The more informative question вЂ” how well does the model forecast in normal times, across many short windows вЂ” is what rolling-window and pseudo-real-time evaluation procedures address. We return to those methods in Chapter 5.
crumbs: 2В  Chapter 2: Decomposition, Smoothing, and Business Cycle Analysis

title: 2В  Chapter 2: Decomposition, Smoothing, and Business Cycle Analysis
text:
2.8 Looking Ahead
The tools developed in this chapter serve as a practical foundation that will be enriched considerably in the chapters that follow.
Chapter 3 provides the theoretical justification for much of what we have done here on intuitive grounds. Simple exponential smoothing is the optimal forecast for an ARIMA(0,1,1) process; HoltвЂ™s method corresponds to ARIMA(0,2,2); Holt-Winters corresponds to a seasonal ARIMA model. These are not coincidences вЂ” the entire ETS family sits inside the ARIMA family as a set of restricted special cases. When we fit an ETS model by maximum likelihood, we are implicitly estimating a restricted ARIMA. Understanding the ARIMA representation unlocks better tools for model selection, residual diagnostics, and the construction of prediction intervals. It also provides the formal machinery for the Beveridge-Nelson decomposition, which separates a unit-root process into its permanent and transitory components вЂ” offering a model-based answer to the same trend-cycle separation problem we approached statistically in this chapter, with no free smoothing parameter and a cycle uniquely pinned down by the estimated ARIMA model.
Chapter 4 addresses a limitation implicit throughout this chapter: all our methods assume the data-generating process is stable over the sample. If the trend growth rate of GDP changed structurally вЂ” as it did in the early 1970s productivity slowdown, and arguably again after 2008 вЂ” then a single HP filter or Holt-Winters model estimated over the full sample will fit neither regime well. Structural break tests, and the methods for handling them, belong to Chapter 4.
Chapter 5 provides the formal framework for evaluating the forecasts produced in this chapter. The train-test exercise above uses RMSE as a loss function, but that is only one of many possibilities. The Diebold-Mariano test asks whether two competing forecasts have statistically different accuracy. Forecast combination вЂ” averaging ETS with ARIMA with HP-based projections вЂ” often outperforms any individual method. And proper calibration of prediction intervals requires checking their empirical coverage over rolling windows. All of this infrastructure is in Chapter 5.
crumbs: 2В  Chapter 2: Decomposition, Smoothing, and Business Cycle Analysis

title: 2В  Chapter 2: Decomposition, Smoothing, and Business Cycle Analysis
text:
2.9 Key Terms






NoteGlossary



Decomposition вЂ” The representation of a time series as a combination of trend, seasonal, and irregular components. Additive: \(y_t = T_t + S_t + I_t\). Multiplicative: \(y_t = T_t \times S_t \times I_t\), equivalent to additive on logarithms.
Trend \(T_t\) вЂ” The slow-moving, long-run level of a time series, abstracting from seasonal and irregular fluctuations.
Seasonal component \(S_t\) вЂ” Systematic, calendar-driven fluctuations that repeat with fixed periodicity \(m\) (4 for quarterly, 12 for monthly data).
Irregular component \(I_t\) вЂ” The residual after trend and seasonal variation are removed; in macroeconomics, the component that contains the business cycle signal.
Classical decomposition вЂ” Trend estimation via a centered \(2 \times m\) moving average, followed by averaging de-trended observations by season to estimate fixed seasonal factors.
Business cycle вЂ” The deviation of real output from its trend (potential) path. Not a natural object in the data, but a residual whose shape depends on the decomposition chosen.
HP filter вЂ” The Hodrick-Prescott filter; extracts a smooth trend by minimizing a penalized sum of squares balancing fit against smoothness, governed by \(\lambda\). Conventional choices: \(\lambda = 1600\) (quarterly), \(\lambda = 14400\) (monthly).
Hamilton filter вЂ” A regression-based cycle filter: project \(y_{t+h}\) on four lags of \(y_t\); the residual is the cycle. Avoids the spurious cycle generation and end-point bias problems of the HP filter.
Output gap вЂ” The percentage deviation of actual GDP from potential (trend) GDP; the cyclical component of a GDP decomposition. The target variable for monetary and fiscal stabilization policy.
Seasonal adjustment вЂ” The removal of the seasonal component from a time series, leaving trend and irregular variation. Official US seasonal adjustment uses X-13-ARIMA-SEATS.
X-13-ARIMA-SEATS вЂ” The Census BureauвЂ™s software for official seasonal adjustment, combining the X-11 iterative moving-average filter with ARIMA-based series extension and (optionally) the SEATS model-based signal extraction procedure.
STL вЂ” Seasonal-Trend decomposition using Loess; a flexible, robust decomposition method that estimates trend and seasonal components using locally weighted regression, allowing seasonal factors to evolve slowly over time.
Loess вЂ” Locally weighted polynomial regression; fits a low-degree polynomial to a local neighborhood of observations at each point, producing a smooth curve that adapts to local structure without imposing a global functional form.
Exponential smoothing вЂ” A family of forecasting methods that form weighted averages of past observations, with geometrically declining weights on observations further in the past.
Smoothing parameter \(\alpha\) вЂ” The weight placed on the most recent observation in an exponential smoothing update; \(\alpha \in (0,1)\). Large \(\alpha\): fast adaptation. Small \(\alpha\): slow adaptation.
Simple exponential smoothing (SES) вЂ” Exponential smoothing for series with no trend and no seasonality; corresponds to ETS(A,N,N) and to the optimal forecast for an ARIMA(0,1,1) process.
HoltвЂ™s linear method вЂ” Exponential smoothing with a separate smoothed trend component; generates linear \(h\)-step-ahead forecasts; corresponds to ETS(A,A,N).
Damped trend вЂ” A modification of HoltвЂ™s method that multiplies the trend by \(\phi \in (0,1)\) at each horizon, causing forecasts to converge to a finite level rather than growing linearly. Corresponds to ETS(A,Ad,N).
Holt-Winters model вЂ” Exponential smoothing with level, trend, and seasonal components; additive version is ETS(A,A,A); damped-trend version is ETS(A,Ad,A).
ETS framework вЂ” A unified taxonomy of exponential smoothing models parameterized by Error type (A or M), Trend type (N, A, Ad, M, Md), and Seasonal type (N, A, M); provides likelihood functions for parameter estimation, AIC for model selection, and analytical prediction intervals.
Prediction interval вЂ” A range \([L_{T+h}, U_{T+h}]\) within which \(y_{T+h}\) is expected to fall with a specified probability; accounts for future forecast uncertainty, not parameter uncertainty.
AIC вЂ” Akaike Information Criterion; \(-2\ell + 2k\) where \(\ell\) is the log-likelihood and \(k\) is the number of parameters; lower is better; used for model selection within the ETS family.
Beveridge-Nelson decomposition вЂ” A decomposition of an \(I(1)\) process into a random walk (permanent) component and a stationary (transitory) component, using the ARIMA representation of the series. Covered in Chapter 3.
crumbs: 2В  Chapter 2: Decomposition, Smoothing, and Business Cycle Analysis
